coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots hybrid membranes and its sorption for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in mixed polymetallic solution
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fengxia
Pang, Bowen
Yang, Tianrui
Liu, Junsheng
coredata.dc:description: With the rapid development of new–energy batteries, heavy-metals pollution from spent batteries has drawn much attention. Various techniques are recently proposed to delete or eliminate such pollution. Among which, membrane sorption exhibits exceptional advantages for the separation of multi-metal solution. For such a purpose, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) hybrid membranes were fabricated via sol–gel technique and hydrothermal synthesis, in which the precursor powder of N-GQDs was obtained by the combination of urea (used as a nitrogen source) and citric acid (used as a carbon source). Subsequently, the N-GQDs precursor was mixed with silane and PVA to generate N-GQDs hybrid membranes (NHMs). Competitive sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in the mixed polymetallic solution, such as a mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) ternary solution, a mixed Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III) quintuple solution, was investigated. It indicated that Cu(II) sorption on NHMs had better selectivity in these mixed polymetallic solutions. Moreover, sorption mechanism of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) demonstrates that the sorption of NHMs for Cu(II) and Pb(II) abided by Elovich model, indicating the properties of heterogeneous chemisorption. Such a trend shows that the sorption via chemical process between the active sites on the top-layer of NHMs, and Cu(II) and Pb(II) is one of the dominant interfering factors. Desorption testing demonstrated that EDTA-2Na is a best de-sorbent for Cu(II) sorption on NHMs and the number of cycles of these membranes can reach up to 10 times. These findings suggest that NHMs can be used to adsorb Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) from mixed polymetallic solution, indicating promising applications in the successive separation of wastewater containing multiple mixed toxic metals from spent new-energy batteries and other metal-containing wastewater.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots hybrid membranes and its sorption for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in mixed polymetallic solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy, Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, 99 Jinxiu Road, Hefei 230601, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy, Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, 99 Jinxiu Road, Hefei 230601, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid development of new–energy batteries, heavy-metals pollution from spent batteries has drawn much attention. Various techniques are recently proposed to delete or eliminate such pollution. Among which, membrane sorption exhibits exceptional advantages for the separation of multi-metal solution. For such a purpose, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) hybrid membranes were fabricated via sol–gel technique and hydrothermal synthesis, in which the precursor powder of N-GQDs was obtained by the combination of urea (used as a nitrogen source) and citric acid (used as a carbon source). Subsequently, the N-GQDs precursor was mixed with silane and PVA to generate N-GQDs hybrid membranes (NHMs). Competitive sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in the mixed polymetallic solution, such as a mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) ternary solution, a mixed Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III) quintuple solution, was investigated. It indicated that Cu(II) sorption on NHMs had better selectivity in these mixed polymetallic solutions. Moreover, sorption mechanism of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) demonstrates that the sorption of NHMs for Cu(II) and Pb(II) abided by Elovich model, indicating the properties of heterogeneous chemisorption. Such a trend shows that the sorption via chemical process between the active sites on the top-layer of NHMs, and Cu(II) and Pb(II) is one of the dominant interfering factors. Desorption testing demonstrated that EDTA-2Na is a best de-sorbent for Cu(II) sorption on NHMs and the number of cycles of these membranes can reach up to 10 times. These findings suggest that NHMs can be used to adsorb Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) from mixed polymetallic solution, indicating promising applications in the successive separation of wastewater containing multiple mixed toxic metals from spent new-energy batteries and other metal-containing wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decades, various new–energy batteries, electronic, mining and mineral processing industries have achieved fast growth, water contamination caused by metal-bearing wastewater is getting more and more serious. For instance, the fabricationoflithium-ionbatteries and the recovery of spent lithium-ionbatteries will produce numerous cobalt-containing wastewater [1,2]. Likewise, the treatment of spent lead–acid batteries will cause waste lead-ions pollution [3,4]. In addition, in IT industry, the resources reutilization of waste printed circuit boards (WPCBs) will also produce different polymetallic toxic metal effluents, such as mixed copper and lead solution [5]. These toxic metal ions are serious threating to the health of human body and ecosystem environment. Hence, the treatment of toxic metal ions from these contaminants has become a hot topic issue around the world [6–8] and is challenging researchers and manufacturers. Hopefully, different practical techniques have been established to resolve such a problem in recent years. They mainly include chemical precipitation [9], electro-dialysis [10], in-situ reduction [11], coagulation [12], nano materials deposition [13–20], membrane filtration [21] and adsorption [22,23]. Among these methods, sorption indicates much benefits and has attracted growing attention due to its simple operation, no secondary pollution, and low cost by comparison with the above-named methods [24–28]. Thus, various kinds of sorbents, such as activated carbon [29], MOF [30], metal oxide [31], and nano-composite [32], have been recently devised and characterized. They display prospective application in the elimination of toxic metal ions from metallic wastewater. However, these sorbents also show several processing problems such as poor selectivity, low sorption capacity, shorter operation life, and decreasing cycle times. Such demerits impede their further utilizations in large-scale metallic wastewater. Especially, it is difficult to use them to treat diluted mixed polymetallic media. Compared with other methods, the outstanding advantages of membrane sorption is that it can be used to treat diluted polymetallic solution through step-by-step manner. Simultaneously, the pollutants can be cleaned via membrane filtration, i.e., sorption and membrane filtration are combined into one step. Accordingly, pure metals can be acquired and recycled. Thus, process flow will be highly shortened. Consequently, continue exploring is still vital.

Currently, study on N-GQDs materials becomes attractive [33–35]. These N-GQDs materials indicate outstanding properties and can be used in such fields as energy, sensors and environment [36]. Specifically, N-GQDs materials is able to eliminate organic pollutants from wastewater by sorption procedure [37,38]. Unfortunately, little work is done up to now to eliminate toxic metal ions from polluted water using N-GQDs materials as adsorbents. Thus, the present research is unsatisfactory.

Recently, in our laboratory, various hybrid-membrane adsorbents were synthesized for the separation of toxic metals from water by sorption [39–41]. In the earlier job, researches chiefly centered on the elimination of toxic metal ions from a single–metal or mixed polymetallic system via membrane sorption. Our continuing hobby in such studies pushes us to do more. To continue such a job, herein, a new kind of N-GQDs hybrid membrane adsorbents were conceived and fabricated. Their sorption natures for Cu(II) ions are assessed via some representative theoretical equations. Differentiating from the earlier job [39–41], the innovative idea of this way is that: (1) the functional groups were directly attached on the molecular chains of hybrid membranes via a sol–gel reaction between N-GQDs and silane, (2) as representative toxic heavy-metals, competitive sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in a mixed polymetallic solution, such as in a mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) ternary solution, and in a mixed Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III) quintuple solution, was inspected using these NHMs as membrane adsorbents, and (3) competitory mechanism of N-GQDs hybrid membrane adsorbents for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) was molded using representative theoretical equations. It is expected that this job can expand the systematical exploration of competitive sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in the mixed polymetallic solutions using NHMs as adsorbents. Especially, this type of research will obtain some fundamental data for the step-by-step separation of mixed-metal wastewater and facilitate the development of adsorptive separation of polymetallic solution.

Citric acid was purchased from Yantai Shuangshuang Chemical Co., Ltd (Shandong, China). Urea (H2NCONH2) was purchased from Hongyan Reagent Factory of Tianjin (Tianjin, China). N–[3–(trimethoxysilyl)propyl] ethylene diamine (TED, purity: ≥ 95.0%) was purchased from Silicone New Material Co., Ltd. of Wuhan University (Wuhan, China). Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, molecular mass: 1750 ± 50) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd of China (Shanghai, China) and used as received. Glutaraldehyde [GA, OCH(CH₂)3CHO, 25%] and other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.
In view of the steps described in articles [34,35], the fabrication of NP can be depicted in detail as below:

First, 0.21 g (ca. 1 mmol) citric acid (used as a carbon source) and 0.18 g (ca. 3 mmol) urea (used as a nitrogen source) were mixed in 5 mL water and agitated strongly for 1 h at room temperature to make the sol–gel reaction. A clear and homogeneous solution could be gotten. Second, the above–prepared clear solution was joined into a reactor, while the reaction temperature was elevated to 160 °C to make hydrothermal synthesis reaction. After that, the reaction temperature was retained at 160 °C for extra 4 h. Third, the reaction production was air-cooled to room temperature and a yellow NP solution was acquired. Finally, the yellow NP solution was air-dried at 40 °C for 6 h, thus a dark powder of NP could be gained. The preparing steps can be schematically presented in Scheme 1 .

First, specified NP powder was melted in 10 mL water and stirred powerfully for 10 min at room temperature until it was resolved completely. Thus a NP solution could be gained. Second, to form a mixed NP-PVA solution, 50 mL 5% (weight percentage, the same as below) PVA solution was added into the as–prepared NP solution and agitated forcefully for 1 h. Third, 2 mL 25% GA, 0.05 mL TEOS, and 0.5 mL TED were added into the as–prepared mixed NP-PVA solution and stirred for extra 4 h to achieve the hybrid precursor, in which GA was able to cross-link with –OH groups from NP (containing –OH and –COOH), PVA (containing –OH) and –SiOH from the chains of TEOS (containing –SiOH) and TED (containing –SiOH, –NH2 and –NH–). Cross-linked network structure was thus created by cross-linkage effect. Subsequently, such a hybrid precursor was put into an ultrasonic cleaner at a frequency of 100 KHz for 20 min so that the bubble in the solution can be removed. And then the de-bubbling solution was aged for additional 12 h. Later, the casting solution of hybrid membrane could be gained. Followed, the casting solution was coated onto a PTFE (i.e., polytetrafluoroethylene) plate and was air-dried at 40 °C for extra 12 h to create a membrane on the PTFE plate, and then they were cooled to room temperature and the membrane was taken off from the PTFE plate. Thus, the NHMs could be obtained.

Similarly, varying the compositions of NP, GA, TED and TEOS in the specimens, a series of NHMs (which were labelled as A0, B1, C3, D5, E4, F3, G2, respectively) can be fabricated and the constituents of these NHMs A0-G2 are tabularized in Table 1 , in which from A0 to D5, the composition of NP in these membranes indicates an rising trend. Instead, from D5 to G2, the amount of TED in these membranes shows a sinking trend.
UV spectra of specimens was done using a TU-1901UV–VisSpectrophotometer (PERSEE, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd. China) in the range of 200–800 nm.

X–ray diffraction (XRD) of specimens was done using a TD–3500 (Dandong Tongda, China) diffractometer under the conditions of Cu Kα radiation at 36 kV and 20 mA. The testing 2θ angle was in the scope of 10 to 60° at a scanning speed of 5°/min.
Raman spectrum was detected in the scope of 700–2450 cm−1 using a DXR (ThermoFisher) laserconfocalmicroRamanspectrometer.
Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectra of membranes A0-G2 were gained through a Shimadzu FTIR–8400S spectrometer in the area of 4000–400/cm at a resolution of 0.85/cm.
TGA and DSC thermal degradation process of NHMs were investigated using a PerkinElmer STA6000 thermo-gravimetry analyzer, at a heating speed of 20 K/min from 30 to 600 °C.

Surface FE–SEM and EDS images were detected through a schottky field emission scanning electron microscope (FE–SEM) (ZEISS GeminiSEM 500) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) (Ultim Extreme), using an activating voltage of 3.0 kV.
Specific surface area, pore volume and pore size were determined on a Quantachrome Autosorb-IQ automatic surface area and pore analyzer.
XPS spectra (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250) were used to confirm the combining of functionalized groups in GHMs.
AFM analysis was conducted using a DI Innova Atomic Force Microscope.

To search the sorption properties of Cu(II) on the NHMs in a mixed polymetallic solution, the sorption experiment was done, in which CuCl2, Co(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2 and Cr(NO3)3 (40 mL, 50 mg/L) were used to obtain a monometallic and mixed polymetallic solution. The original concentrations of metal ions were altered from 10 to 100 mg/L. To adjust the pH value, 1.0 mol/L sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were used so that the pH of solution can keep at 5. The sorption capacity (qMe in mg/g) of metal ions on the NHMs can be calculated from Eq.(1).
(1)
q
Me
=
C 0 - C R
V
W
where initial and remaining metal concentrations are labelled as C0 and CR (mg/L), respectively; the volume (L) of solution is labelled as V; the weight of membrane is labelled as W (g).

To study the reuse and recycle times of NHMs, de-sorption experiment was carried out, in which HCl, HNO3 and EDTA-2Na (0.2 mol/L) were selected as de-sorbents. The de-sorption steps can be seen as follows. First, 0.2 g NHMs A0-G2 were dipped into 50 mg/L mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) solution for 12 h. Followed, to remove the excess metal ions on the surface of membranes, the membranes were taken out and washed with ultra-water. And then, the adsorbed membrane specimens were put into 0.2 mol/L HCl, HNO3 and EDTA-2Na solution (40 mL) for 12 h. Subsequently, the residual metal concentration was detected. Next, the above-used desorbed membrane specimen was dipped into ultra-water for 4 h so that the extra metal ions on the surface of membranes were cleaned. Finally, such desorbed membrane was used to carry out another recycle testing of sorption and desorption.
Fig. 1 is the comparison of color of the initial and exposed on ultraviolet light at 365 nm of NP solution.

As shown in Fig. 1, the initial color of NP solution is yellow. As it was exposed on ultraviolet light, its color was changed into bright-blue fluorescence. Such a phenomenon is agreement with the result reported in an article [42], signifying that NP solution was successfully synthesized. To describe such a trend, much attention should be paid to the excellent properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Generally, GQDs can be credited to zero dimensional materials and have exceptional edge effects. For example, GQDs indicate various features including redox, photo-induced electron transfer and fluorescence characteristics [43]. Especially, the bright-blue fluorescence can simply be detected at 365 nm as they were exposed on ultraviolet light. Therefore, bright-blue fluorescence is the intrinsic and representative features of GQDs. in view of this trend, a conclusion can be drawn that the above-prepared precursor solution has the performances of GQDs and is a representative N-GQDs material.
To further confirm the fluorescence characteristics of yellow solution aforesaid, UV absorption spectrum was detected and is presented in Fig. 2 .

As shown in Fig. 2, the biggest value of ultraviolet wavelength is located in 365 nm. If the NP powder was dissolved in water, a dark-blue color emerged from the solution. As this solution was exposed on ultraviolet light at 365 nm, bright-blue fluorescence can also be found. That is to say, the fluorescence characteristic of NP solution was left intact even if it was air-dried at 40 °C to powder state.
Fig. 3 illustrates the XRD pattern of NP powder.

The larger diffraction peak appeared at near 2θ = 26.9° is the diffraction peak of crystal face (002) of graphene, i.e., the NP powder exhibits the structure of graphene crystal. This finding is agreement with the result reported in an article [36]. Moreover, the d-spacing value (d) of crystal face can be estimated using Bragg equation [44] as Eq. (2):
(2)
n λ = 2 d sin θ
in which, λ is the wavelength of the electrons, n is the order of diffraction, an integer, d is the spacing of the crystal planes and θ the angle of incidence.

In view of diffraction peak (002) of graphene, the d-spacing value (d) of crystal face is d = 0.333 nm. Considering this finding, a conclusion can be made that the NP powder has the crystal structure of graphene. Such a finding further ascertains that the specimen as-produced is the GQDs material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size distribution and zeta potentials of NP powder
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) and (b) is the size distribution and zeta potentials of NP powder.

As given in Fig. 4(a), the size of NP powder mainly sited in around 396 nm. Two significant peaks appeared at −130 mV and 42.5 mV [see Fig. 4(b)]. Such a conclusion demonstrates that NP powder discloses the negatively-charged features, suggesting that it has excellent stability [45]. Note that, such negatively-charged properties of NP powder will be conducive to the sorption of metal ions on its surface owing to the existences of electrostatic interaction between them.
To study the combination of valence bond in NP powder, XPS examination was implemented and the XPS spectra of NP powder are offered in Fig. 5 .

As displayed in Fig. 5(a), the peaks in XPS Survey at 285.7 eV、400.1 eV、531.2 eV can be credited to core element C、N、O, respectively. In Fig. 5(b), the C1s core-level spectrum verifies the existence of carbon–carbon single bond (C-C, or C-H ∼ 284.8 eV), carbon–oxygen single bond (C-O or C-N, ∼286.2 eV), carbon–oxygen double bond (C=O, ∼287.7 eV), and carboxylic acid groups (O-C=O, ∼288.9 eV). In addition, in Fig. 5(c), the N1s peak located at 400 eV can be ascribed to the N from pyrrole. As indicated in an article [46], the main existing state of N is pyrrole nitrogen (pyrrole-N) in N-doped graphene. Especially, pyrrole-N has special optical properties, and it can make graphene produce bright-blue fluorescence. This is the reason why N-GQDs material can emit blue fluorescence at 365 nm. Furthermore, pyrrole-N sites are considered as the properties of preferent-ially adsorbing cations [34], thus it will give N-doped graphene excellent electronic and ionic conductivity. in Fig. 5(d), the O1s peak placed at 531.6 eV can be ascribed to carbon–oxygen double bond (C=O) from –COOH.
These results infer that NP powder has the crystal structure of graphene.
To detect the position of functional groups on the molecular chains of NP, FTIR spectrum of NP powder was conducted and is presented in Fig. 6 .

The peaks sited at 3407 and 3175 cm−1 can be allocated to the symmetric and anti-symmetric stretching vibration of N-H and –OH groups, which are located within the scope of 3500–3050 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, the band at neighboring 1400 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of C-N bond. In addition, the peak sited at 1586 cm−1 is the peak of C=C bond, which is conformable to the observation in XPS of C1s spectrum.
Fig. 7 is the Raman spectrum of NP powder.

As shown in Fig. 7, the peaks sited at 1342 and 1566 cm−1 are the D and G peaks of graphene, respectively; in which D peak suggests the structure of amorphous carbon. In contrast, G peak means the structure of ordered graphite carbon [47,48]. Generally, the defect level of graphene can be determined by the comparison of the intensity of D and G peaks, i.e., the ratio of ID/IG. It is well known [34,35] that the bigger of ID/IG value, the greater the degree of edge defect is. Taking NP powder into considered, the ID/IG value is equal to 0.89, suggesting the presence of more defects in the structure and higher degree of graphitization. Such performances of more defects and higher graphitization degree will be favorable to the electrostatic sorption of toxic metal cations. As a result, the sorption capacity and removal efficiency of toxic metal ions will be accordingly enhanced.

Normally, the quantity of raw material in an adsorbent will influence its property. To optimize the components in NHMs, the effect of the quantity of raw material in NHMs on their sorption for toxic metal ions was studied and is listed in Table 2 .

As shown in Table 2, different compositions of raw material in NHMs can impact their sorption capacities for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II). For instance, the incorporation of NP into the NHMs can clearly increase the sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) (see membrane I, and V in Table 2). Similarly, the addition of GA into the NHMs will also increase the sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) (see membrane II, and V in Table 2). Instead, the increasing amount of TED into the NHMs can elevate the sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) (see membrane IV, and V in Table 2). But the doping of TEOS in the NHMs will cause a slight rise in the sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) (see membrane III, and V in Table 2). However, if their ratios of components into these membranes are appropriate, an increase in the sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) will be obtained (cf. membrane V in Table 2). This finding suggests that the quantity of starting material into the NHMs is of vital importance. In view of this analysis, the compositions of NHMs were optimized (see Table 1, hereinbefore).

To determine the influence of both NP and TED on the inner pore structure of NHMs, the surface area, pore volume and pore size of these membranes A0, B1, C3, D5, E4, F3 and G2 were measured and are listed in Table 3 .

As shown in Table 3, the BET surface area of NHMs is much smaller. While, the BET surface area rises with an increase in NP content in membranes A0, B1, C3, and D5. Nevertheless, both SBJH and VBJH values all drop with the addition of NP. Conversely, the pore size shows different changing trends. Taking membranes A0, B1, C3, and D5 as example (in which the NP content increased from A0 to D5), DBJH follows such a trend: B1 > C3 > A0 > D5. Namely, the pore size increases firstly and then decreases as the NP is added. But the pore volume shows an opposite trend as the NP is added. In contrast, for membranes D5, E4, F3 and G2 (in which the TED content decreased from D5 to G2), BET surface area and DBJH reveal different trend. For example, the BET surface area increases with an increasing TED content in these membranes. Among them, the BET surface area of D5 indicates the largest value. However, pore size of D5 shows the lowest value. Obviously, this result suggests that the combination of NP and TED is conducive to the increase in surface areas and shrinkage in pore size of NHMs.
To continue detect the functional groups in the hybrid membranes, FTIR spectra of NHMs A0-G2 were scrutinized and are given in Fig. 8 .

As presented in Fig. 8, the strong peak at 3262 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of –OH group [24]. The peak sited at 2933 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of –CH2 group. Moreover, the band at near 1653 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of N–H bond [24]. In addition, the peak sited at 1326 cm−1 is the peak of C-N bond. The strong peak at 1084 cm−1 is the overlapping of stretching vibration of Si–O–Si and C–O–C bonds. The weak band at 914 cm−1 is the bending vibration of –C–H bond. The band related to Si–C was found at 830 cm−1. In addition, the band at 830 cm−1 was disappeared for membrane C3, i.e., with the increase composition of NHMs, the cross-linking reaction of groups was occurred. Similar phenomenon can also be found for membrane F3. These change trends in FTIR spectra suggest that the crosslinking reaction between the groups has happened.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TGA and DSC curves of NHMs
ce:section.ce:para: To detect the thermal degradation actions of these hybrid membranes, TGA and DSC analyses of NHMs were measured and the relevant TGA and DSC curves are presented in Fig. 9 (a) and (b). Meanwhile, the thermal analysis data gotten from these TGA and DSC curves are listed in Table 4 .

As shown in Fig. 9(a), their change trends of membranes A0-G2 in TGA curves are alike and two stages are detected. However, for the single membrane, the decreasing extent is different. For instance, membrane A0 has large weight loss (%) (WL %) within the temperature scope of 254–500 °C. The temperature of WL percentage increased with the growing amount of N-GQDs precursor (NP) into membranes A0-D5, signifying that the doping of NP into the membranes can increase their thermal stabilities. The reason can be ascribed to the graphene structure of N-GQDs. Moreover, for membranes E4-G2, their trends in TGA curves are resemble. This is because they had the same composition of NP, only the amount of TED decreased somewhat, namely, such a slight change in TED ingredient cannot alter their thermal stabilities.

Furthermore, in Fig. 9(a), the degradation temperature (DT) at 5 and 10 % WL (i.e., DT5 and DT10) discloses different changing tendencies (cf. Table 4). Taking membranes A0-D5 as instances, the DT5 values specify a descending trend with the increasing composition of NP. Besides, the DT10 values indicate a rising trend with the increasing composition of NP. Instead, as the composition of NP in membranes E4-G2 was fixed, the DT5 and DT10 values all show rising trends as the composition of TED was decreased. These findings imply that the amount of TED doesn’t change the DT of hybrid membranes. Such a trend is consistent with the observation in TGA curves. Thus, it can be deduced that the composition of NP into these hybrid membranes will have large influence on their thermal stabilities.

From Fig. 9(b), the DSC curves of membranes A0-G2 are also resemble. However, the Tg value of membranes A0-G2 indicates a descending trend as NP was doped into these membranes. This finding proves that the doping of NP into these membranes is able to increase their flexibilities. In addition, in DSC curves in Fig. 9(b), only one exothermic peak is detected. The theoretical explanation to such a trend can be seen as below. As testified in an article [46], in DSC curves, the only one or multiple endothermic points are the melting temperature of crystal; but the exothermic peak is the crystallizing temperature. In view of these DSC curves, a conclusion can be made that the crystallizing temperature (CT) is near 138 °C. Characteristically, with the addition of NP into these hybrid membranes, the peak temperature of A0, B1, C3 and D5 is positioned within the scope of 135–143 °C and such a temperature drops a little. These outcomes validate that the doping of NP into these hybrid membranes doesn’t alter the molecular structure.
Fig. 10 presents the relationship of stress and strain (%) of NHMs.

It can be discovered that within the strain scope of 0–5%, the stress of these hybrid membranes increases violently and reaches the maximal value, and then they drop sharply until fracture occurs. Remarkably, among these NHMs, membrane A0 (without adding NP) has the lowest stress and the value is 26.2 MPa. However, membrane A0 shows the longest strain and reaches up to 63.8%. Such a phenomenon suggests that the stress and strain of membrane A0 indicates an opposite trend. However, such an opposite trend will be highly changed as the addition of NP into the hybrid membranes. Taking membranes B1, C3 and D5 as typical examples, their stresses increase sharply with the growing composition of NP and then drop quickly. In addition, the stress of membranes B1, C3 and D5 indicates a descendant trend. This finding suggests that proper adding of NP can increase the stress of membrane and excess adding of NP will drop its stress. Moreover, as the composition of NP into the hybrid membranes is fixed, the stress of membranes E4, F3 and G2 increases from E4 to F3 and then drops from F3 to G2. That is to say, appropriate doping of TED into the hybrid membranes can also increase its stress and excess doping of TED will decline its stress. Clearly, the stress of these hybrid membranes can be adjusted via the appropriate regulation of NP and TED, providing a new direction to adjust the stress of hybrid membranes.
To search the binding of functionalized groups in NHMs, XPS spectra were performed and the relevant signal curves are illustrated in Fig. 11 (a)-(e).

From Fig. 11(a), the peaks in XPS Survey situated at 284.8, 102.2, 399.6 and 532.3 eV can be assigned to the core element C, Si, N, and O, respectively. Note that, compared these peaks with those observed in Fig. 5(a), it is clearly found that new peak at 102.2 eV was appeared. This result confirms the implement of combining reaction among functional groups from NP with these from membranes. In addition, as shown in Fig. 11(b), the C 1s core-level spectrum of membranes also indicates the presence of carbon–carbon single bond (C-C, or C-H ∼ 284.8 eV), carbon–oxygen single bond (C-O, ∼286.0 eV), and carboxylic acid groups (O-C=O, ∼287.8 eV). Meanwhile, it can be seen that the C-O bond appears in B1, demonstrating the combination of functional groups with other groups (such as –OH and Si–OH) as the incorporation of NP into membranes. Besides, from Fig. 11(c), their change trends of Si2p are similar and the stronger peak intensity-es appear at near 102 eV (102.45, 102.05, 102.2, 102.1, 102.00, 102.00 and 101.95 eV for membranes A0, B1, C3, D5, E4, F3 and G2, respectively), suggesting the linking between functional groups and the formation of O–Si–O bond in these membranes. As shown in Fig. 11(d), the N1s peak placed at 399.6 eV can be ascribed to carbon–oxygen double bond (C-N) from C–NH2 and C–NH–C in the molecular chains. From Fig. 11(e), the O1s peak placed at 532.3 eV can be ascribed to carbon- oxygen double bond (C=O) from –OH and –COOH.

To study the surface-e topography of NHMs AFM analysis was performed and the relevant AFM 3D images are illustrated in Fig. 12 (a)-(g). Meanwhile, to explore the surface feature-peak height information, the mean (Hmean) and maximum (Hmax) feature-peak heights of these membranes A0, B1, C3, D5, E4, F3 and G2 are given in Fig. 12(h).

As shown in Fig. 12(a)-(g), the surface of membranes A0, B1, C3, D5, E4, F3 and G2 is a normal hills and valleys morphology [49,50]. The feature-peak height indicates some differences. For example, from membranes A0, B1, C3, and D5 (in which the NP content increased from A0 to D5), Hmax increases and reaches the maximum value (155.43 nm) for membrane C3, and then it noticeably decreases for membrane D5 (70.299 nm). Besides, the Hmean shows similar trend and the maximum value is 66.125 nm for membrane B1. This result suggests the addition of NP changes the surface morphology. But, for membranes E4, F3 and G2 (in which the TED content decreased from D5 to G2), Hmax decreases with a decrease in TED content. Interestingly, for membrane D5, its content of NP and TED is the highest among these membranes. However, the Hmax of membrane D5 doesn’t reveal the highest value. Such a finding demonstrates that proper addition of NP and TED in these membranes can increase their compatibility and adjust its surface morphology. The reason can be assigned to the filling of valleys on the surface, causing a drop of feature-peak height as the ratio of NP and TED in these membranes is reasonable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II)
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the competitive sorption activities of NHMs for different toxic metal ions, sorption experiment was implemented using Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) as main representatives of heavy-metal ions. The sorption capacities and selectivity properties of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) onto these hybrid membranes were also assessed, and the regeneration cycle performance was examined. Meanwhile, to continue corroborate the existence of adsorbed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) onto these NHMs, surface FE–SEM and EDS images are observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in a monometallic solution

ce:section.ce:para: Generally, pH value is a significant interfering factor on the sorption of NHMs for toxic heavy-metal ions, because it will produce sediment of toxic metal ions as the pH value is larger than 7 in the alkaline condition. To scrutinize the effect of pH on the sorption of toxic heavy-metal ions, the interfering effect of pH value on the sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in a monometallic solution was carried out within the pH scope of 2–6 and the relevant curves are presented in Fig. 13 (a), (b) and (c).

As illustrated in Fig. 13(a), for the single membrane, at pH = 5, the sorption capacity of Cu(II) ions could be arrived at the maximal value. As the pH is lower than 3, the sorption capacity of Cu(II) decreases quickly. The reason can be credited to the protonation of functional groups on the surface of NHMs. Thus these protonated functional groups will produce electrostatic repulsion effect on Cu(II) ions. As a result, the sorption capacity of Cu(II) ions is reduced within the pH scope of 2–3. Contrariwise, as the pH is larger than 5, the sorption capacity of Cu(II) ions also drops rapidly. This is because the formation of sediment of copper and accordingly it will decrease the sorption capacity of Cu(II) ions. Moreover, for membranes A0-G2, the change trends in sorption capacity of Cu(II) ions are various. For instance, the sorption capacity of membranes A0-D5 for Cu(II) ions indicates a rising trend, i.e., the sorption capacity of Cu(II) ions increases with the growing composition of NP from membrane A0 to D5. This finding suggests that the adding of NP into these hybrid membranes is favorable to the sorption of Cu(II) ions. Likewise, the sorption capacity of Cu(II) ions from membrane G2 to E4 also reveals a rising trend, i.e., the growing amount of TED into these hybrid membranes is also conducive to the sorption of Cu(II) ions. The cause can be attributed to the complex effect of –NH2 and –NH– groups from TED with Cu(II) ions. Thus it is easily drawn a conclusion that the doping of both NP and TED will be favorable to the sorption of NHMs for Cu(II) ions.

As shown in Fig. 13(b) and (c), the maximal sorption capacity of Co(II) and Pb(II) on these hybrid membranes all occurred at pH = 5. Besides, the change trends of NHMs for Co(II) sorption are alike to the sorption of Cu(II) ions on these membranes, but the sorption capacity of Co(II) is highly lesser than that of Cu(II) ions. As for the sorption of these membranes for Pb(II), their change trends are different from those of Cu(II), and Co(II). Considering A0-D5, the sorption capacity of Pb(II) shows a descending trend, i.e., the addition of NP is unfavorable to the sorption of Pb(II) on these hybrid membranes. Instead the sorption capacity of membranes G2- E4 for Pb(II) discloses an rising trend, i.e., the doping of TED in these hybrid membranes is helpful to increase the sorption capacity of Pb(II). The category of functional groups on the surface of hybrid membranes will be responsible for such trends. Furthermore, by comparison of the sorption capacity of NHMs A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II), it can be discovered that they abide by such a sequence as: Cu(II) > Pb(II) > Co(II) except that on membrane A0. Particularly, for membrane A0 (without adding NP), the sorption capacity abides by such a sequence as: Pb(II) > Cu(II) > Co(II).
In view of the above results, the optimum pH value for the sorption of NHMs for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) was chosen as pH = 5.
To realize the sorption speed of toxic metal ions on NHMs A0-G2, the effect of contact time versus sorption capacity of toxic metal ions was determined and is given in Fig. 14 (a), (b) and (c).

As Fig. 14(a), (b) and (c), for NHMs A0-G2, the sorption capacity of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) all increased with the elapsed time and reached the equilibrium state. However, for the single membrane, the change trend is various. Especially, for Cu(II), in the first 4 h, the sorption capacity rises quickly. And then it increases slowly at 6 h and reaches a saturation state at around 8 h. For Co(II), the sorption capacity increases speedily in the first 2 h. And then it increases slowly at 4 h and reaches a saturation state at 8 h. Regarding Pb(II), in the first 4 h, the sorption capacity increases quickly. And then it reaches a saturation state at 12 h.

By comparison of the sorption capacity of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II), different tendencies are observed. Among membranes A0-G2, membrane D5 has the largest sorption capacity of Cu(II) and Co(II), suggesting that the adding of NP in these hybrid membranes was favorable to the sorption of Cu(II) and Co(II). The reason can be ascribed to the complex effect of functional groups from NP with Cu(II) and Co(II) ions. Contrarily, membrane A0 has the largest sorption capacity of Pb(II), signifying that the doping of NP in these hybrid membranes makes little contribution to intensifying the sorption of Pb(II).

To discover the effect of initial concentration on the sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in a monometallic solution, sorption experiment was done and the initial concentration was altered from 10, 20, 50, 80 to 100 mg/L. Fig. 15 (a), (b) and (c) illustrates the function of initial concentration versus sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II).

As shown in Fig. 15(a), (b) and (c), for the single membrane, the sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) all increases with the raised initial concentration of solution. These phenomena are logical if the amounts of heavy-metal ions in solution are considered. However, for membranes A0-G2, their change trends are different. For example, the sorption capacity of membranes A0-D5 for Cu(II) ions increases with the growing content of NP into these membranes within high concentration region of 50–100 mg/L. Besides, the sorption capacity of membranes E4-G2 for Cu(II) ions also increases with the growing content of TED in these hybrid membranes within higher concentration of 50–100 mg/L. However, within lower concentration of 10–20 mg/L, such a difference is decreased. Moreover, the trends in the sorption capacity of Co(II) on membranes A0-G2 are similar to those observed in Cu(II) ions. But, the sorption capacity of Pb(II) on membranes A0-G2 decreases with the growing content of NP into membranes A0-G2 within the concentration of 20–100 mg/L. Furthermore, the sorption capacity of Pb(II) increases with the growing content of TED in membranes E4-G2. The reason can be ascribed to an increase in the distance from heavy- metal ions to the functional groups and the prolong of combining time.

Solution temperature is a chief interfering factor on the sorption of toxic heavy-metal ions on NHMs. Fig. 16 (a), (b) and (c) is the relationship of solution temperature versus sorption capacity of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II).

As presented in Fig. 16(a), (b) and (c), the sorption capacity of these membranes for Cu(II), and Co(II) rises with the raised solution temperature in the area of 25–60 °C, signifying that the sorption of Cu(II), and Co(II) on these membranes is an endothermic process. Nevertheless, the sorption of Pb(II) on these membranes specifies diverse trends. For instance, the sorption capacity of Pb(II) on membranes A0-G2 increased with the elevated solution temperature in the scope of 25 to 45 °C and reached the largest value at 45 °C. Subsequently, they declined as the solution temperature passed through 45 °C except that of membrane D5. For membrane D5, the sorption capacity of Pb(II) reached the largest value at 55 °C. Undoubtedly, solution temperature at 45 °C is more appropriate for the sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on NHMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in mixed polymetallic solution
ce:section.ce:para: To survey the sorption natures of NHMs for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in different mixed polymetallic solution, the sorption capacities of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) ternary solution, and mixed Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III) quintuple solution were explored and the relevant plots are depicted in Fig. 17 (a) and (b).

As exposed in Fig. 17(a), the sorption behaviors of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) onto these membranes indicates different altering trends. For instance, sorption capacity of Cu(II) on these membranes have the largest values among these divalent metal ions. Specifically, the sorption capacities of Cu(II) on NHMs B1-G2 are all larger than those on membrane A0 (without adding NP), suggesting that the adding of NP into these membranes is favorable to Cu(II) sorption. Among them, membrane B1 has the largest sorption capacity for both Cu(II) (9.03 mg/g) and Co(II) (2.17 mg/g). Instead, membrane A0 has the highest sorption capacity for Pb(II) (4.17 mg/g). Such trends further validate that the doping of NP in these hybrid membranes can favor the sorption of Cu(II) and Co(II), but it will be adverse to the sorption of Pb(II). These trends can be further proved via Surface FE-SEM and EDS images (cf. Fig. 18 ).

As shown in Fig. 17(b), the sorption of metal ions on these NHMs all shows various change trends. For instance, for membranes B1- G2, the sorption capacity of these metal ions abides by such a sequence as: Cu(II) > Pb(II) > Ca(II) > Co(II) > Cr(III). However, for membrane A0 (without adding NP), the sorption capacity of these metal ions abides by such a sequence as: Cu(II) > Pb(II) > Cr(III) > Ca(II) > Co(II). Among these membranes, membrane B1 reveals better sorption performances for Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III). Thus, choosing membrane B1 as a representative example, sorption capacity of Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III) on membrane B1 was 7.4, 3.8, 2.6, 2.2, and 0.19 mg/g, respectively. Undoubtedly, the sorption capacity of Cu(II) is about 1.95 times larger than that of Pb(II) and around 2.85 times higher than that of Ca(II). While, the sorption capacity of Pb(II) is near 1.46 times larger than that of Ca(II) and about 1.73 times larger than that of Co(II). That is to say, Cu(II), and Pb(II) have good sorption selectivity on membrane B1. In contrast, the sorption of Cr(III) has poor selectivity on membrane B1. In view of these results, it can be inferred that mixed polymetallic ions can be separated via successive method using these N-GQDs membranes, designating promising application in the recycle of multiple mixed-metal ions from the spent new-energy batteries and other polluted water.

Two dominating factors might be accountable for such trends. One is the electrostatic effect of metal cations with the negatively-charged surface of hybrid membrane (as confirmed by zeta potentials in Fig. 4b), which will rise the attraction between metal ions such as Cu(II) with surface functional groups such as –NH2 or –COOH groups. The other can be allotted to the synergistic effect of NP with TED, which will be conducive to the creation of hybrid matrix and be helpful to the blending of metal ions with functional groups.

Sorption selectivity is an effective tool to evaluate the property difference of an adsorbent in a mixed polymetallic solution system. As representative instances, the sorption performances of NHMs A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) was investigated in a monometallic and mixed polymetallic solution, in which the initial concentration is kept at 50 mg/L at pH = 5. The distribution coefficient (Kd) and sorption selectivity coefficient (SSC) were estimated via Eqs. (2) and (3) [51–53] in view of the results from a competitive sorption (see Fig. 3):

where C0 and CR are the initial and remaining metal concentrations (mg/L), respectively; V is the volume of solution (L);W is the weight of sample (g); Kd is the distribution coefficient (L/g); Kd Cu is the Cu(II) distribution coefficient; Kd C is the competitive ionic distribution coefficient.

The distribution coefficient Kd and selectivity coefficient SSC of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in a monometallic solution and mixed polymetallic solutions are tabularized in Table 5 , which was calculated from Fig. 17.

As shown in Table 5, both Kd and SSC disclose various change trends in a monometallic solution and mixed polymetallic solution. For instance, in the monometallic solution system, membrane A0 had the largest Kd value for Pb(II). Whereas, membranes B1-G2 had the highest Kd values for Cu(II). In contrast, in the mixed polymetallic solution systems, membranes A0-G2 all had the largest Kd values for Cu(II). These results demonstrate that Cu(II) has excellent distribution in these NHMs. Moreover, the SSC value of these NHMs also indicates different change trends. For example, in a mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) ternary solution, SSC value of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II)/ Co(II) abided by such a sequence as: membrane C3 > G2 > D5 > B1 > F3 > A0 = E4, i.e., membrane C3 had the highest SSC value for Cu(II)/ Co(II). Meanwhile, SSC value of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II)/ Pb(II) abided by such a sequence as: B1 > C3 > G2 > F3 > D5 > A0 = E4, i.e., Membrane B1 had the highest SSC value for Cu(II)/ Pb(II). But, in a mixed Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III) quintuple solution, new trends appeared. SSC value of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II)/ Co(II) abided by such a sequence as: A0 > B1 > C3 > F3 > E4 > G2 > D5, i.e., Membrane A0 had the highest SSC value for Cu(II)/ Co(II). SSC value of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II)/ Pb(II) abided by such a sequence as: B1 > C3 > A0 > G2 > F3 > E4 > D5, i.e., Membrane B1 still had the highest SSC value for Cu(II)/ Pb(II).

In addition, if the Kd values of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II) in the mixed Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III) quintuple solution system were compared with those in the mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) ternary solution system, it is interesting to find that the Kd values of membranes B1-G2 for Cu(II) in the mixed Cu(II), Pb(II), Ca(II), Co(II) and Cr(III) quintuple solution are all larger than those in the mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) ternary solution except that of membrane A0, validating the existence of competitory relationship among these metal ions. Regarding membrane A0, the Kd values for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in these mixed polymetallic solutions are similar, signifying that the distribution of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in the mixed polymetallic solution has little influence by competing metals. In view of both Kd and SSC data, a conclusion can be drawn that Cu(II) sorption on these NHMs has excellent sorption selectivity.

Since membrane B1 had the largest sorption capacity among these NHMs in the mixed polymetallic solution. Thus, membrane B1 was chosen as a representative specimen to observe the surface change. The surface FE-SEM and EDS images of the initial and the adsorbed membrane B1 in the mixed Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) ternary solution were illustrated in Fig. 18.

In Fig. 18(a), the surface of the initial membrane B1 is smooth and tight. Instead, some particles emerge on the surface of the adsorbed membrane B1 [Fig. 18(b)]. Meanwhile, the EDS image of the adsorbed membrane B1 [Fig. 18(c)] clearly confirms the existence of Cu, Co and Pb elemental peaks on its surface. Conversely, Cu, Co and Pb peaks are not observed on the EDS image of the initial membrane B1 [Fig. 18(d)]. Undoubtedly, these surface FE–SEM and DES images provide further corroborations to support the existence of completion among Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) sorption on these NHMs.

Regeneration and reuse of an adsorbent are very vital for its industrial application. To explore the recycle efficiency of these NHMs, as a representative instance, sorption and de-sorption testing of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II) were done, in which HCl, HNO3, and EDTA-2Na were used as emblematic de-sorbents. The relevant results are illustrated in Fig. 19 (a)-(c).

As presented in Fig. 19(a)-(c), among these de-sorbents, HCl, HNO3, and EDTA-2Na, EDTA-2Na specifies high de-sorption efficiency and cycle times, signifying that EDTA-2Na is a good de-sorbent for these toxic metal ions on these membranes. While, for the single membrane, de-sorption efficiency is different. Among them, the sorption capacity of membranes A0 and B1 keeps relatively stable after 10 time cycles of sorption and de-sorption using EDTA-2Na as a de-sorbent. This result suggests that the doping of NP into these membranes doesn’t lessen their regeneration cycle times. However, for acidic de-sorbents HCl, and HNO3, the cycle time was highly reduced. The reason can be attributed to the high solubility of metal ions in strong acidic environments. Contrarily, EDTA-2Na has larger binding ability with metal ions.

It should be emphasized that compared with our previous job and similar researches, the cycle times are highly increased (see Table 8, hereinafter), demonstrating excellent reuse ability of these membranes.

To study the sorption mechanism of NHMs for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II), experimental results were analyzed via some traditional theoretical equations, such as Lagergren pseudo–first–order (LP1O) and pseudo–second–order (LP2O) kinetic equations, intra–particle diffusion (IPD) equation, Elovich model (EM), as well as Langmuir, Freundlich and Temkin isotherm equations, which will be discussed as below.

Lagergren kinetic model can be categorized as Lagergren pseudo-first-order (LP1O) and pseudo-second-order (LP2O) kinetic equations [48,51], which can be simply written as linear equations. (4b) and (5b), respectively.

in which k1 (1/h) and k2 (g/h mg) are the LP1O, and LP2O rate constant, respectively; qt and qe (mg/g) are the q value of metal ions [Me(II)] at contact time t (h) and at an equilibrium state, respectively.

In view of LP1O and LP2O kinetic equations, the sorption kinetic models are easily obtained by modeling the data of sorption time versus sorption capacity of NHMs A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II). The relevant matching curves are presented in Fig. 20 (a), (b) and (c). The model parameters are listed in Table 6 .

As shown in Table 6, LP1O model of NHMs A0-G2 shows poor correlation for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) sorption. Meanwhile, larger difference exists in the tested qexp and the calculated qcal. Nevertheless, LP2O model of NHMs A0-G2 has better matching degree, especially for Cu(II). Moreover, the calculated qcal values from LP2O equation are close to the tested qexp values. To further examine the fit quality, as a typical example, the non-linear chi-square (χ2) values and Akaike information criterion (AIC) of LP2O were also recorded in this table, in which chi-square (χ2) value can be calculated by Eq. (6) [54] and Akaike information criterion (AIC) is easily obtained by Eq. (7) [24,55].

in which N is the number of tested points, Np the number of model parameters, yi,exp and yi,model the experimental and calculated data.

Clearly, the chi-square (χ2) values and AIC of LP2O equation are very lower. In view of R2 adj, χ2 values and AIC data, it is reasonable to infer that LP2O equation can be applied to evaluate the sorption behavior of NHMs A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II).

Moreover, as presented in Table 6, for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II), the change trends of second- order rate constant (k2) and initial sorption rate (ISR) are diverse. For instance, for Cu(II) sorption on membranes A0-D5, the k2 values indicate a descending trend from A0 to D5. However, the ISR values of membrane A0-D5 doesn’t follow such a trend. Membrane B1 has the highest ISR among them, signifying that the addition of NP into the membranes can increase the ISR. But excessive addition of NP into the membranes will decrease the ISR of Cu(II). Similarly, for Co(II) sorption on membranes A0-D5, the k2 values also shows a descending trend from A0 to D5. However, the ISR increases from membrane A0 to C3 and then decreases from D5 to F3, i.e., membrane C3 has the highest ISR among them. Regarding Pb(II) sorption on membranes A0-D5, the k2 values reveal a rising trend from A0 to D5. But the ISR increases from membrane A0 to C3 and then decreases from C3 to D5. Such results denote that the adding of NP into the membranes can elevate both the second-order rate constant (k2) and ISR of Pb(II). Notice that, by comparison of the initial sorption rate (h) of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on NHMs A0-D5, the ISR of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on membrane A0 follows such an order as: Cu(II) (4.228) > Pb(II) (2.365) > Co(II) (0.638). Contrariwise, the ISR of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on membrane B1 follows such a sequence as: Cu(II) (4.977) > Pb(II) (3.317) > Co(II) (0.808). Clearly, the addition of NP into the membranes increases the ISR of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II). The reason can be ascribed to an increase in the amount of functional groups, which will produce larger electrostatic complex effect on metal ions. Based on these findings, a conclusion can be made that the adding NP into the membranes is favorable to the sorption of toxic heavy-metal ions on these membranes.

Furthermore, Considering TED, the doping of TED into these membranes doesn’t produce such a lifting effect on sorption of membranes for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II). For instance, for Cu(II) on membranes D5-G2, the k2 values have small changes from D5 to G2 except F3, indicating that the adding of TED into these membranes has little influence on the sorption of Cu(II). Contrarily, the ISR values of membrane D5-G2 follow such a trend as: D5 > E4 > G2 > F3, i.e., the ISR values decreases with the decreasing composition of TED. That is to say, the increase in the amount of TED into these membranes can increase the ISR of Cu(II).

Although LP1O and LP2O kinetic equations can be used to predict the sorption behaviors of NHMs A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II), it is difficult to obtain the diffusion information of toxic heavy-metal ions across membranes. Hence, the modeling of sorption time versus sorption capacity of toxic metal ions using IPD equation is necessary. As reported in references [52,53], it is able to describe the transportation performances of metal ions from a medium into particle pores through the interface between the medium and the adsorbent. IPD equation usually is expressed as linear style:
(8)
q t
=
k p
t
1 2
+
x i
in which qt (mg/g) is the adsorbed amount at time t (h), kp (mg/g h1/2) is the IPD rate-constant, and xi (mg/g) is the intercept of straight line.

It was reported [39] that if the diagram of qt versus t1/2 is a straight line and passes through the origin, the sorption process is considered as an IPD mechanism. Otherwise, such a sorption process will be impacted by other controllable steps.
Fig. 21 is the IPD curves of NHMs A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) sorption. The relevant model parameters are summarized in Table 6.

As shown in Fig. 21, the sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) onto NHMs A0-G2 is non-linear over the range of sorption time, and the linear matching degree of NHMs A0-G2 is lower (see Table 6). Meanwhile, the matching straight line of NHMs A0-G2 all does not pass through the origin (i.e. intercept xi ≠ 0). This result demonstrates that IPD is not the only rate-controlling process. More controllable steps are involved.

Normally, Elovich equation is used to describe the kinetics of heterogeneous chemisorption of metal ions on a solid surface [56,57]. The relevant Elovich parameters can be simply written as linear equation (9).
(9)
q t
= a ln t + b
where a and b (mg/g) are the Elovich parameters. Among them, parameter a can be easily got from the slope of matching straight line, and parameter b can be acquired from the intercept of matching straight line.
Fig. 22 displays the linear matching of EM of NHMs A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II). The parameters both a and b, as well as correlation values for the EM are listed in Table 6.

As illustrated in Fig. 22, the linear curves of NHMs A0-G2 have well-matching with the EM (cf. Table 6), representing that Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) sorption on these membranes can be described using EM. Notice that, by comparison of these kinetic models for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) sorption, their changing trends are various.

Furthermore, for the single membrane, the changing trend is also different. For instance, for Cu(II) sorption onto NHMs A0-C3, the R2 adj values of LP2Omodel are all higher than the R2 adj values of EM, suggesting that Cu(II) sorption on membranes A0-C3 abides by the LP2Omodel. Conversely, for Cu(II) sorption onto NHMs D5-G2, the R2 adj values of EM are all higher than the R2 adj values of LP2O model, indicating that Cu(II) sorption on membranes D5-G2 abides by EM.

Currently, Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherm models are three representative models to evaluate the sorption performances of heavy metals on the interface of a solid-state sorbent. Among them, Langmuir isotherm (LI) equation is considered as a monolayer sorption on a homogeneous adsorbent surface [58–61], which usually is written as linear equation (10):
(10)
Ce
q e
=
1
Q m
C e +
1
Q m
b
where qe (mg/g) and Ce (mg/L) are the equilibrium concentrations of metal ions in the adsorbed phase and liquid part. Both Qm (mg/g) and b (L/mg) are the Langmuir constants. According to Ce/qe against Ce, constant b can be obtained from the intercept of straight line, and Qm is simply got from the slope of the same straight line.

Fig. 23 presents the matching curves of sorption data of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on membranes A0-G2 using LI equation. The related parameters are listed in Table 7
.

Differencing from LI equation, Freundlich isotherm (FI) equation indicates the heterogeneous surface sorption with non-uniform energies of active sites [58–61], which usually is expressed as equation (11):
(11)
q e
=
k F
C e
1 n
where qe (mg/g) and Ce (mg/L) are the equilibrium concentrations of metal ions in the adsorbed phase and liquid state, respectively. Both kF [(mg/g) (mg/L)−1/n] and n are the FI constants, they are able to acquire from the non-linear curve fitting of qe against Ce.

Fig. 24 (a), (b), and (c) illustrates the matching curves of sorption data of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on membranes A0-G2 using FI equation based on a non-linear curve fitting method. The relevant parameters are tabularized in Table 7.

Temkin isotherm (TI) model is used to evaluate the sorption heat, which usually is expressed as linear equation (12): [62,63].
(12)
q e
=
RT
b T
ln
(
K T
C e
)
=
B T
ln
(
K T
C e
)
=
B T
ln
(
K T
)
+
B T
ln
(
C e
)
where constant BT = RT/bT, which is related to the sorption heat, R is the gas constant (8.314 J/mol K), T is the absolute temperate (K), bT is the TI constant, which is the variation of sorption energy (KJ/mol) and KT is the equilibrium binding constant corresponding to the maximum binding energy. Both BT and KT can be calculated from the slope and the intercept of the linear plot in view of qe vs. ln(Ce), respectively.
Fig. 25 (a), (b), and (c) shows the matching curves of sorption data of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on membranes A0-G2 using TI model. The relevant parameters are tabularized in Table 7.

From Table 7, it is easily found that for single toxic heavy-metal ions, the correlation coefficient (R2 adj) is different. For example, for Cu(II) sorption, the model data on membrane A0 indicates best matching with FI model. Whereas, for membranes B1 to G2, these data fitted well with TI model. In view of these results, a conclusion can be made that FI equation is a good isotherm model to describe Cu(II) sorption on membrane A0, i.e., Cu(II) sorption on membrane A0 is a heterogeneous surface sorption. Conversely, TI equation is an excellent isotherm model to describe Cu(II) sorption on membranes B1-G2. As for Co(II) sorption, membrane A0 shows poor matching with these three models, suggesting that Co(II) sorption on membrane A0 cannot be described by LI, FI, and TI equations. Instead, membranes B1-F3 have good matching with LI equation, representing that Co(II) sorption on these membranes is a representative mono- molecular layer process. Regarding Pb(II) sorption, membrane A0 reveals best matching with LI equation, denoting that Pb(II) sorption on membrane A0 is a representative mono-molecular layer process. However, Pb(II) sorption on membranes B1-G2 indicates good matching with FI equation, suggesting that Pb(II) sorption on membranes B1-G2 is a heterogeneous process.

The above phenomena can be explained as below, the electrostatic effect between functional groups on membrane surface and toxic heavy-metal ions is one of the chief reasons. For membrane A0, it contains relatively high amount of NP (containing –OH and –COOH) and doesn’t contain any TED (containing –NH2 and –NH–), thus the sorption effect mainly comes from the –OH and –COOH groups on NP. Instead, membranes B1-G2 contain not only NP, but also TED. As a result, complex-effect of heavy-metal cations with –OH and –COOH groups from NP and –NH2 and –NH– groups from TED will be produced and sorption effect will be altered. So, the sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on membranes B1-G2 shows various behaviors. The proposed sorption mechanism can be illustrated in Scheme 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: A comparison of sorption property and cycle times of NHMs for metal ions with different sorbents.
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the sorption property of NHMs for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in this study with various sorbents reported in references [62,64–68] for metal ions, their Langmuir maximum adsorption capacities (Qm) and recycle times are tabulated in Table 8.

From Table 8, it can be seen that the sorption capacity of Cu(II) on membrane D5 is larger than those reported in references and our previous work. While, the recycle times of such a membrane are also highly increased. Besides, the sorption capacity of Co(II) is larger than that reported in our previous work. However, sorption capacity of Pb(II) is greatly lower than those reported in references. Three main factors will be responsible for such trends. One can be ascribed to the difference of experimental conditions. Another can be assigned the difference of effective sorption sites. For membrane sorption, the sorption primarily occurs on the external surface of membrane, and the sorption effect from inside membrane pore is relatively lower. The third reason is related to the category of functional groups, which will produce diverse complex ability.

In brief, NHMs are excellent membrane sorbents. Their chief merits are the selective sorption for toxic heavy-metal ions from mixed polymetallic solution, indicating promising application in the step-by-step separation of industrial wastewater.

New nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) hybrid membranes were prepared and characterizations. Their sorption behaviors for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) in mixed polymetallic solutions were examined. Especially, competitory sorption of Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) on these membranes was entirely studied. The results showed that the sorption of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II) in the mixed polymetallic solutions had excellent sorption selectivity by comparison with Co(II) and Pb(II). Sorption mechanism reveals that the sorption of membranes A0-G2 for Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) abided by different models. These findings reveal that NHMs can be potentially applied to selectively remove Cu(II), Co(II) and Pb(II) from mixed polymetallic solution via successive strategy.
